title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Fall for Second Consecutive Week Along with Decrease in Type 1 Transfer Volumes
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights: (25th â€“ 31st August 2025)
  • A total of 60,683 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a 39.04% fall week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a significant rise of152.07% WoW as 460,399 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $54.96/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 2.67% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers declined by 2.26% in price, hitting $56.14/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 0.58% WoW; on 2nd September, the spot price stood at $56.13/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $58.63/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices increased by 0.93% WoW. D4 prices remained unchanged, while D5 and D6 prices rose by 0.95% and 1.89% WoW respectively.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

In the California LCFS market, credit prices fell for a second consecutive week accompanied by a dip in Type 1 transfer volumes. In the wider fuels space, Californiaâ€™s Energy Commission voted to temporarily set aside penalties for excessive refining profits that were adopted after gasoline pump prices climbed over $8 a gallon in 2022. Californiaâ€™s Democratic Governor Gavin Newsom had earlier proposed the penalties, but has switched direction amidst worries of price spikes in 2026 after the closure of the Phillips 66 refinery and a San Francisco-area plant operated by Valero Energy Corp in the coming year. Both companies stated that declining gasoline demand promoted by stateâ€™s policies in favour of non-fossil-fuel-powered vehicles, such as the California LCFS, made the once-lucrative California market untenable in the long-term.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_603051" align="alignnone" width="771"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 2.67% WoW decline, falling from $56.47 to $54.96 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices decreased by 2.26% WoW, falling from $57.44 to $56.14. Type 1 trades witnessed 60,683 credits exchanged, reflecting a 39.04% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw an increase of 152.07% WoW, reaching 460,399 credits.  

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_603053" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 0.58% WoW, reaching $56.30/MT. On 2nd September, the spot price was $56.13/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $58.63/MT as of 2nd September.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_603054" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 35 trades were reported in the market, with 6 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $77.35 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 25,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $44.09 per credit of one Type 2 transaction reported for 50,000 credits. The largest volume of 4 transfers involving 50,000 credits each, for Type 2 transactions at $44.09, $53.79, $57.61 and $58.00. The smallest volume of transfer involved 1,000 credits for a Type 2 transaction priced at $56.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_603056" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $56.47 in August so far, up from $47.99 in July. Trade volumes for the month of August stood at 1.93M as of 2nd September, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_603055" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 25th â€“ 31st August, total VPT recorded a fall of 1.36% WoW, with a fall in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.

Â Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 0.93% WoW. D4 prices remained unchanged, while D5 and D6 prices rose by 0.95% and 1.89% WoW respectively. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming datesÂ 

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • California Sets Aside Penalties for High Refinery Profits: Californiaâ€™s Energy Commission voted to temporarily set aside penalties for excessive refining profits that were adopted after gasoline pump prices climbed over $8 a gallon in 2022.Californiaâ€™s Democratic Governor Gavin Newsom had proposed the penalties, but has since switched direction amid worries of price spikes in 2026 after the closure of the Phillips 66 refinery and a San Francisco-area plant operated by Valero Energy Corp next year. Both companies said declining gasoline demand promoted by stateâ€™s policies in favor of non-fossil-fuel-powered vehicles made the once-lucrative California market untenable in the long-term.
  • John Deere Announces B30 Biodiesel Compatibility Across Engine Portfolio: John Deere announced the approval of B30 biodiesel use across its entire portfolio of Tier 4 John Deere engines. This increases the previous approval of blends up to B20. The announcement will also be highlighted this week at the 2025 Farm Progress Show in Decatur, IL, where all John Deere equipment operated in the companyâ€™s Ride and Drive area will run on B30 biodiesel. â€œThis is a win-win situation for U.S. soybean farmers and rural communities,â€ said Caleb Ragland, President of the American Soybean Association and Kentucky soybean farmer.
  • World's First 2025 Model Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle Debuts in Prince Rupert: A new hydrogen fuel cell truck is now being tested at the Port of Prince Rupert as part of the Heavy Duty Zero Emissions Vehicle Pilot Project, a partnership with Innovate BC's Integrated Marketplace. The 2025 model truck is the first of its kind anywhere in the world to be deployed, and Prince Rupert gets first dibs. Prince Rupert Fire Rescue joined the Prince Rupert Port Authority for a hands-on safety training session with Hyundai Motor Group to learn about the new vehicleâ€”the 2025 Hyundai XCIENT Fuel Cell Truckâ€”which will be locally operated by Innovate BC's program partners Gat Leedm Logistics drivers and tested on a route between Prince Rupert and Terrace.
